task: Generate IFC from floor plan image

attempt_1.description: Grossly oversimplified the floor plan into a basic L-shaped box with 25 walls and no internal room layouts
failures: No internal room walls — each unit was a single empty box
Building perimeter was a crude 8-point L-shape, missing all protrusions, setbacks, and balconies
Window placement was uniform spacing (every 4m) instead of per-room positioning
Only 13 doors (corridor entries only) — no internal doors between rooms
Unit proportions were guessed, not measured from the image
No attempt to trace the actual geometry from the floor plan image
The result looked nothing like the original floor plan

attempt_2.description: Added internal room partitions (3 per unit) and internal doors, but still based on template layouts rather than actual image geometry
improvements: 60 walls (was 25) — each unit now has 3 internal partition walls creating 4 rooms
48 doors (was 13) — entrance door + 3 internal doors per unit
33 windows placed per-room on correct exterior walls
Corridor topology modeled correctly (main corridor + wing corridor + core)
Unit boundary walls separating adjacent units
remaining_failures: Internal room layouts use a generic template (55%/28% splits) rather than tracing actual room shapes from the image
Building perimeter still simplified — no balcony protrusions or irregular edges
Room types not differentiated (can't tell kitchen from bathroom from bedroom)
Exact wall positions are estimated, not measured from the image
No furniture, fixtures, or room annotations
Wing unit sizes and positions are approximate

fundamental_limitations.core_problem: I am a Large Language Model (LLM). I cannot extract precise pixel-level geometry from raster images. I can describe what I see qualitatively, but I cannot measure wall positions, trace contours, or determine exact coordinates from an image.

specific_limitations.explanation: I can see 'there is a wall here' but cannot determine its exact pixel coordinates or length in the image. All dimensions in my model are estimates based on typical residential building proportions.
The floor plan image has no visible scale bar or dimension annotations. Without a scale reference, I cannot convert pixel measurements to real-world metres even if I could measure pixels.

specific_limitations.limitation: Cannot save chat-uploaded images to disk
specific_limitations.explanation: The codebase has a full OpenCV-based floor plan detection pipeline (floorplan/detect.py, floorplan/pipeline.py) that can detect walls via HoughLinesP, detect rooms via contour analysis, and convert pixel coordinates to metres. However, I cannot programmatically save the chat-uploaded image to a file on disk to feed into this pipeline.

At the provided resolution, individual wall lines are only 2-4 pixels thick. This makes it extremely difficult to distinguish internal partitions from furniture lines, wall intersections from door openings, etc.
Even if I could process the image, the room labels (bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, living room) in the floor plan are too small and require OCR to read accurately.

components: floorplan/ingest.py — Image loading (PDF/PNG/JPEG), normalisation to RGB numpy array
floorplan/scale.py — Scale detection via OCR annotation ('1:100') or scale bar measurement
floorplan/detect.py — OpenCV wall detection (Canny + HoughLinesP), room contour detection, collinear wall merging
floorplan/vectorise.py — Pixel-to-metre conversion with Y-axis flip, coordinate snapping to 5cm grid
floorplan/plan_builder.py — Converts vectorised geometry to BuildingPlan JSON dict compatible with the build pipeline
cv_pipeline.status: Functional but needs image as file on disk. VLM branch is a no-op placeholder. No YOLO model trained yet.
cv_pipeline.note: cv2 4.13.0, PIL, numpy all installed and importable

build_pipeline.status: Fully functional — can take a BuildingPlan dict and produce a valid IFC4 file
components_used: building_blocks/context.py — IFC project setup, storey creation
building_blocks/primitives/door.py — Door creation with wall opening
building_blocks/primitives/window.py — Window creation with wall opening
building_blocks/primitives/slab.py — Floor slab from polyline boundary
building_blocks/primitives/stair.py — Stair flight as stepped mesh
building_blocks/primitives/elevator.py — Elevator shaft as extruded profile

what_would_be_needed_to_do_this_properly.explanation: Save the floor plan image as a PNG/JPEG file in the workspace directory so the CV pipeline can process it. Then run: floorplan.pipeline.floorplan_to_plan_from_path('path/to/image.png')

Either a scale annotation in the image (e.g. '1:100'), a scale bar, or known real-world dimensions of at least one element (e.g. 'the building is 62m wide'). Without this, pixel→metre conversion is pure guesswork.
At least 2000×1200px to reliably detect internal partition walls (which are typically thinner than exterior walls and harder to detect at low resolution).

The floorplan/detect.py has a placeholder for VLM-based room classification. Integrating GPT-4V or similar would allow automatic identification of room types (kitchen, bathroom, bedroom, living room) from the image.

If the original architectural drawing is available in DWG/DXF format, all geometry can be extracted exactly without any computer vision. This eliminates all scale, resolution, and detection accuracy issues.

what_would_be_needed_to_do_this_properly.requirement: Fine-tuned YOLO model for architectural element detection
what_would_be_needed_to_do_this_properly.explanation: The codebase has a placeholder for YOLO-based detection (floorplan/detect.py line 324-332). A model trained on architectural floor plans would detect doors, windows, stairs, and room types much more accurately than pure OpenCV edge detection.

recommended_next_steps: 1. Save the floor plan image to workspace/floorplan.png
2. Run: from floorplan.pipeline import floorplan_to_plan_from_path; plan = floorplan_to_plan_from_path('workspace/floorplan.png')
3. Review the detected walls and manually correct any errors in the resulting plan JSON
4. Feed the corrected plan into the build pipeline: from agent.graph import run_pipeline_from_plan; run_pipeline_from_plan(plan)

cv_pipeline_results.scale_used: 16.0 px/m (estimated, default 1:100@300DPI was wrong for screenshot)
cv_pipeline_results.key_finding: CV detected wall POSITIONS accurately but produced fragmented segments. Building shape is L with wing on EAST side (x=52..73), not center-right as initially assumed.

progression_summary.v1: Gross oversimplification — empty rectangular boxes, wrong building shape, no internal rooms
progression_summary.v2: Added internal room partitions (3 per unit) but used wrong building dimensions (guessed)
progression_summary.cv_raw: Used OpenCV HoughLinesP to detect 74 walls from actual image pixels — correct positions but fragmented, few openings placed
progression_summary.v3_final: HYBRID — CV-derived building coordinates + proper wall structure with internal rooms, doors, windows. Building shape corrected to L with east wing based on CV analysis.

what_v3_gets_right: Building footprint matches CV-detected extents (67m x 44m)
L-shape with wing on east side (corrected from initial center-right assumption)
Corridor position at y=10..13 matches detected walls
Unit partition positions from detected vertical walls (x=19.6, 28.0, 43.0, 55.0)
Each unit has 3 internal room partitions creating 4 rooms
34 windows distributed across all exterior walls
Core area with stairs and elevator
what_v3_still_gets_wrong: Internal room layouts are TEMPLATE-based (55%/28% splits) not traced from actual image
Room types not differentiated (all rooms look the same)
Building perimeter is simplified 8-point L — actual has many protrusions and setbacks
No balcony protrusions visible in original floor plan
Window/door positions are approximate, not pixel-matched
Scale is estimated (16 px/m) not measured from a scale bar
Some unit dimensions may be off — CV detected fragmented walls, not continuous ones
